title: Strong Average-Case Circuit Lower Bounds from Non-trivial Derandomization
speaker: Lijie Chen
abstract: We prove that, unconditionally, for all constants a, NQP = NTIME[n^polylog(n)] cannot be (1/2 + 2^(-log^a n) )-approximated by 2^(log^a n)-size ACC^0 circuits. Previously, it was even open whether E^NP can be (1/2+1/sqrt(n))-approximated by AC^0[2] circuits.

proof_stages.label: ACC lower-bound and PRG bottlenecks
proof_stages.summary: The talk motivates the main target by tracing AC0-to-ACC0 circuit lower bounds, explaining why strong average-case lower bounds are needed for PRGs, and stating the new NQP average-case lower bound against polynomial-size ACC0-type circuits.

proof_stages.label: CAP implication and consequences
proof_stages.summary: The speaker formulates the circuit acceptance probability problem and states the core implication that a nontrivial CAP algorithm for subexponential-size circuits in a class C yields strong average-case lower bounds for NQP, then derives consequences for ACC0-threshold circuits and white-box versus black-box derandomization.

proof_stages.label: Failure of naive average-case lifting
proof_stages.summary: Known worst-case lower-bound proofs are shown not to extend directly because easy-witness lemmas require worst-case containment, while worst-case-to-average-case amplification is too expensive and needs majority decoders that are not available inside ACC0.

proof_stages.label: MA derandomization template and nondeterministic PRGs
proof_stages.summary: The proof strategy is recast through MA lower bounds: derandomizing MA yields NTIME lower bounds, nondeterministic PRGs suffice for this derandomization, and conditional NPRGs under the assumed false containment are enough for contradiction.

proof_stages.label: Bootstrapping and infinite-often alignment
proof_stages.summary: Williams-style bootstrapping turns nontrivial CAP algorithms into polylog-seed NPRGs, and the infinite-often nature of the resulting generators is handled by replacing almost-everywhere MA hardness with sufficiently dense hard input lengths that intersect the generator’s working intervals.

proof_stages.label: Average-case collapse obstruction for NC1
proof_stages.summary: To run bootstrapping under an average-case assumption, one needs an NC1-to-ACC0-type collapse, but ordinary random self-reduction only handles constant advantage and near-half error correction appears to require majority, recreating the amplification barrier.

proof_stages.label: Approximate linear sums and final contradiction
proof_stages.summary: Approximate linear sums are introduced as a substitute for majority: special NC1 error correctors collapse NC1 to approximate sums of ACC0, these sums preserve nontrivial CAP/derandomization algorithms, and combining this with MA average-case hardness gives the desired NQP strong average-case lower bound and the stated conceptual takeaways.

examples.name: Parity for AC0
examples.note: Parity is recalled as the elementary Boolean function known to be hard for AC0, illustrating the historical strategy of strengthening circuit classes by adding a function they cannot compute.
examples.engagement_reason: The speaker defines parity as odd Hamming weight and cites known AC0 lower bounds, but does not compute or prove anything with it.
